```text
# AI-Native Product Playbook

A Claude Code plugin for AI-native product management workflows. Built for PMs
who use Claude Code as their daily operating system.

## What This Is

A plugin that gives Claude Code new skills for product work — starting with
fetching and structuring Google Docs for local use. Each skill encodes a
repeatable workflow: the mechanical work (API calls, file formatting) lives in
scripts, the judgment (what to surface, what to suggest next) lives in prompts.

## Quick Start

### 1. Install the Plugin

**For Shortform team members** (recommended):

Make sure you're authenticated with GitHub:
```bash
gh auth login
```

Then add the marketplace and install the plugin:
```
/plugin marketplace add shortformhq/ai-native-product-playbook
/plugin install ai-native-pm
```

**Updating the plugin:**

When new skills or improvements are pushed to the repo:
```
/plugin marketplace update ai-native-product-playbook
```
This pulls the latest changes. No reinstall needed.

**Auto-config via project settings:**

If your project repo has `extraKnownMarketplaces` configured in
`.claude/settings.json` (the Shortform product repo does), Claude Code will
prompt you to install the marketplace and plugin automatically when you open
the project. To enable auto-updates, set `GITHUB_TOKEN` in your shell profile:
```bash
export GITHUB_TOKEN=$(gh auth token)
```

**For local development/testing:**

```bash
claude --plugin-dir /path/to/ai-native-product-playbook/plugins/ai-native-pm
```

### 2. Set Up Google Workspace CLI (`gws`)

The `pm:fetch-doc` skill requires `gws` to access Google Docs.

**Install:**

```bash
brew install google-workspace-cli/tap/gws
```

**Authenticate:**

```bash
gws auth login
```

This opens a browser for Google OAuth. Sign in with your work account.

**Enable APIs:**

Go to [Google Cloud Console](https://console.cloud.google.com/apis/library) and
enable these APIs for your GCP project:

- Google Docs API
- Google Drive API

If you don't have a GCP project yet:

```bash
gcloud projects create my-ai-project --name="AI Native Product"
gcloud config set project my-ai-project
```

Then enable the APIs:

```bash
gcloud services enable docs.googleapis.com drive.googleapis.com
```

**Verify setup:**

```bash
gws docs documents get --params '{"documentId": "1BxiMVs0XRA5nFMdKvBdBZjgmUUqptlbs74OgVE2upms"}'
```

If this returns JSON, you're ready.

### 3. Use It

```
/pm:fetch-doc https://docs.google.com/document/d/1ABC.../edit
```

This creates a local directory with the doc's content, organized by tabs,
with comments extracted separately.

## Available Skills

| Skill | Command | Description |
|---|---|---|
| Fetch Doc | `/pm:fetch-doc <url>` | Fetch a Google Doc into a structured local directory with all tabs, comments, and auto-generated outline |

## Output Structure

Every fetched doc produces:

```
{doc-slug}/
  _index.md        <- metadata + outline (read this first)
  tab-{slug}.md    <- one file per tab
  comments.md      <- all comments, open and resolved
```

## Plugin Structure

```
ai-native-product-playbook/
  .claude-plugin/
    marketplace.json         <- plugin registry
  plugins/
    ai-native-pm/
      skills/
        fetch-doc/
          SKILL.md           <- workflow prompt
      tools/
        fetch_doc.py         <- fetch script
      CLAUDE.md              <- plugin context (loaded automatically)
  playbook/                  <- framework docs and tech plans
  README.md                  <- this file
```

## Team Setup (for Shortform)

The product repo's `.claude/settings.json` is pre-configured with:

```json
{
  "extraKnownMarketplaces": {
    "ai-native-product-playbook": {
      "source": {
        "source": "github",
        "repo": "shortformhq/ai-native-product-playbook"
      }
    }
  },
  "enabledPlugins": {
    "ai-native-pm@ai-native-product-playbook": true
  }
}
```

When team members open the product repo in Claude Code and trust the project
folder, they'll be prompted to install the marketplace and plugin automatically.

**For auto-updates** (optional): set `GITHUB_TOKEN` in your shell profile so
Claude Code can pull updates at startup without prompting:
```bash
# Add to ~/.zshrc or ~/.bashrc
export GITHUB_TOKEN=$(gh auth token)
```

## Architecture

This plugin is built on the **agent-native architecture** framework and draws
structural inspiration from the **compound-engineering plugin**. Both are listed
below as foundational references. The plugin's architecture should evolve in
alignment with agent-native principles — if a decision doesn't map cleanly to
one of these patterns, that's a signal to reconsider.

### Core Patterns We Follow

| Pattern | From | How we apply it |
|---------|------|-----------------|
| Files as Universal Interface | Agent-native | All output is structured markdown files — self-documenting, inspectable, no databases |
| context.md | Agent-native | Every fetched doc produces `_index.md` — the entry point Claude reads first to orient |
| Primitives + Prompts | Agent-native | Scripts handle mechanics (`fetch_doc.py`), SKILL.md handles judgment and workflow |
| Shared Workspace | Agent-native | Same `drive/` directory used by humans and Claude — no sandboxing or sync layers |
| Composability | Agent-native | New skills compose on existing output. Future skills read what `fetch-doc` produces |
| Granularity | Agent-native | `gws` commands are atomic primitives; the script is a domain tool; the skill is the prompt layer |
| CRUD Completeness | Agent-native | Currently read-only — create/update/delete are known gaps to fill as needs arise |
| Skills as Workflows | Compound Engineering | Each skill is a SKILL.md with phased execution, prerequisites, and critical rules |
| Plugin Marketplace | Compound Engineering | Distributed as a Claude Code plugin via `.claude-plugin/marketplace.json` |

### What's Not Yet Agent-Native

These are known gaps where we haven't reached full agent-native architecture:

- **No write-back** — we can read Google Docs but can't update them from Claude
  (`gws docs documents batchUpdate` is available when needed)
- **No dynamic capability discovery** — skills are statically defined rather than
  discovered at runtime
- **No explicit completion signals** — skills rely on Claude's judgment rather
  than formal completion/pause/escalation states

### Inspirations

**[Agent-Native Architectures](https://every.to/guides/agent-native)** by Dan
Shipper and Claude. The foundational framework for building applications where
AI agents are first-class citizens. Key concepts: files as universal interface,
parity between human and agent capabilities, atomic primitives composed by
prompts, emergent capability through composability, and CRUD completeness as an
architectural audit tool. This guide is the "why" behind every structural
decision in this plugin.

**[Compound Engineering Plugin](https://github.com/EveryInc/compound-engineering-plugin)**
by Every. The reference implementation for Claude Code plugins at scale — 35+
agents, 40+ skills, 11k+ stars. Structural patterns we adopted: SKILL.md as
phased workflow prompts, `plugins/` directory layout, `marketplace.json` for
distribution, and CLAUDE.md as persistent plugin context. Their philosophy —
"each unit of engineering work should make subsequent units easier" — applies
equally to product work.

## Prerequisites

- Claude Code CLI
- Python 3.8+
- Google Workspace CLI (`gws`) — see setup instructions above
- GitHub access to this repo (for plugin installation)
```
</details></tool-use>

# ai-native-product-playbook

## Contributing

- **All changes require a PR.** Never commit directly to main. Create a topic branch, commit, and open a PR via `gh pr create` in the same session as the edit.
- **Project file organization.** Group related files into named subfolders under `projects/{status}/` (e.g., `projects/in-progress/q2-spec-writing/`). Move the entire folder when status changes.
```
</details></tool-use>

_**Agent (claude-opus-4-6 2026-04-13 11:44:24Z)**_

<tool-use data-tool-type="shell" data-tool-name="Bash"><details>
<summary>Tool use: **Bash**</summary>
Read plugin CLAUDE.md from ai-native repo

`gh api "repos/shortformhq/ai-native-product-playbook/contents/plugins/ai-native-pm/CLAUDE.md" --jq '.content' | base64 -d 2>&1`

```text
# AI-Native PM Plugin

This plugin provides AI-native workflows for product managers. It is designed
around the agent-native architecture framework: files as the universal interface,
atomic primitives composed by prompts, and shared workspaces between humans and
agents.

---

## Available Skills

| Skill | Command | Description |
|---|---|---|
| Fetch Doc | `/pm:fetch-doc <url>` | Fetch a Google Doc (all tabs, comments, outline) into a structured local directory |
| Roadmap | `/pm:roadmap [mode]` | Analyze a TSV roadmap for weekly planning, risk checks, and status updates. Modes: `status` (default), `week`, `risks`, `update`, `launched` |
| Changelog | `/pm:changelog [source] [target]` | Generate a concise, stakeholder-readable changelog between two document versions. Compares fetched Google Doc tabs, local files, or git revisions. |
| Tasks | `/pm:tasks [mode]` | Manage Google Tasks — view status, refresh weekly from roadmap, triage inbox, reorder lists. Modes: `status` (default), `refresh`, `triage`, `reorder`, `complete` |
| Deep Read | `/pm:deep-read <doc-slug>` | Read a document through a persona's lens — surfaces what matters most for that role, connects findings to strategic priorities, identifies opportunities for impact |
| Learn | `/pm:learn` | Capture signal from working sessions to improve personas, charters, and stakeholder scratch files — the compound learning loop |
| Close | `/pm:close` | Session close-out — uncommitted work check, task cleanup, memory capture, handoff context, then learn flow. Use `--quick` to skip learn. |
| Scan Links | `/pm:scan-links <doc-url>` | Extract all hyperlinks from a Google Doc, fetch each linked resource in parallel, and produce a grouped summary report — the bulk-read complement to deep-read |
| Draft Comms | `/pm:draft-comms` | Draft emails, Slack messages, or doc comments in the user's voice — loads a personal style guide and examples corpus before generating |
| Refine Style | `/pm:refine-style` | After sending, diff the draft vs. what was actually sent, extract patterns, create an example file, and propose updates to the style guide |
| Query DB | `/pm:query-db [sql or question]` | Query Shortform's read-replica database — manages SSH tunnel, supports raw SQL and natural-language questions, caches schema locally |
| RICE Prep | `/pm:rice-prep` | Prepare a RICE scoring session — harvest ideas from source docs, de-dup, format pre-read doc with scoring template, generate CSV for live scoring |
| RICE Post | `/pm:rice-post [sheet URL]` | Process completed RICE scoring results — ingest multi-scorer data, flag disagreements, produce force-ranked tiers, generate next steps |
| Critique | `/pm:critique` | Run a multi-stakeholder critique on any artifact — simulates perspectives from loaded personas/scratch files plus a first-principles critic. Use `--inline` for lightweight single-pass review. |
| Daily Report | `/pm:daily-report` | Generate and send a daily product briefing as a 10-message Slack thread — pulls from Calendar, Tasks, Slack, Gmail, GitHub, and Mixpanel |
| Research | `/pm:research <topic>` | Conduct structured external research — runs parallel agents with different angles, synthesizes findings into an actionable brief. Use `--quick` for single-pass. |
| Prep PR | `/pm:prep-pr` | Prepare changes for a pull request — agent review, conciseness check, consistency check, then commit and create PR. **Default for all PRs** — run this before creating any PR unless the user explicitly skips it. |
| Estimate Impact | `/pm:estimate-impact [file]` | Build a bottom-up impact estimate for an experiment or feature — segments the funnel, estimates treatment effects per segment, produces a three-scenario revenue model. Use `--output standalone` for a standalone doc. |
| GitHub Status | `/pm:github-status [mode]` | Read GitHub Projects boards for product visibility — sprint status, story points, what shipped. Modes: `sprint` (default), `quarter`, `launched` |
| Daily Report | `/pm:daily-report` | Generate and send a daily product briefing to Slack DMs — GitHub PRs, sprint status, standups, Gmail, and tasks in one message |
| Write Issue | `/pm:write-issue [flags]` | Create GitHub issues matching engineering standards — assertive language, present tense, context > problem > solution. Flags: `--plan` (include implementation plan), `--from <path>` |
| Sync Plugin | `/pm:sync-plugin` | Pull latest plugin changes into primary repo and Claude Code marketplace install. Run after merging a PR. |
| Prep 1:1 | `/pm:prep-1on1 <person>` | Prepare for a recurring 1:1 — reads the shared notes doc, gathers context from Calendar, Gmail, Slack, GitHub, and memory, synthesizes a prioritized agenda with persona-aware talking points |
| Add to Roadmap | `/pm:add-to-roadmap <url-or-description>` | Research a project or idea from provided resources (GitHub issues, docs, Figma), assess readiness, and add to the Roadmap or Idea Backlog tab of the product roadmap Google Sheet |
| Write GDoc | `/pm:write-gdoc <doc-url-or-id> <file.md> [...]` | Write local markdown files to a Google Doc as formatted tabs — headings, native tables, inline formatting, links, bullets. The inverse of fetch-doc. |
| Prototype | `/pm:prototype [spec-path]` | Generate a self-contained HTML prototype from a spec — pulls production design tokens, builds interactive screens, optionally captures a GIF walkthrough. Use `--gif` for animated walkthrough. |

## Operator Context

The `context/` directory contains structured information about the people and
roles this plugin serves. Skills should use this context to tailor their work.

```
context/
├── charters/
│   └── head-of-product.md     # Role scope, principles, priorities, 90-day plan
├── personas/
│   ├── _template.md            # Reusable template for new role personas
│   ├── head-of-product.md      # Simulatable perspective for autonomous agent work
│   ├── founder-ceo.md          # Full persona — Allen Cheng (Founder/CEO)
│   ├── tech-lead.md            # Full persona — Alan (Tech Lead, Product Pod)
│   ├── product-manager.md      # Full persona — Roman Nevmyvako (PM)
│   ├── founder-ceo-scratch.md  # Raw observations — Allen Cheng (Founder/CEO)
│   ├── product-manager-scratch.md  # Raw observations — Roman Nevmyvako (PM)

│   ├── tech-lead-scratch.md        # Raw observations — Alan (Tech Lead)

### When to Load Context

- **Doing work on behalf of Davis** (drafting, analyzing, reviewing): Load
  `context/personas/head-of-product.md` for his POV — decision heuristics,
  communication style, what triggers pushback, calibration examples.
- **Framing recommendations or proposals**: Reference
  `context/charters/head-of-product.md` for priorities (P0-P3), ownership
  boundaries, success metrics, and what he's explicitly not doing right now.
- **Simulating stakeholder review**: Load multiple personas to produce
  divergent perspectives on the same artifact.
- **After sessions with real decisions**: Follow the compound learning flow
  in `context/continuous-improvement.md` to capture calibration signal.

### How Context Improves

See `context/continuous-improvement.md` for the full process. The short version:
- **Post-session**: Surface calibration candidates, get approval, write to persona
- **Post-project**: Comprehensive persona accuracy check
- **Post-correction**: Targeted evolution log entry when persona diverges from reality
- **Quarterly**: Charter drift detection + example quality audit

---

## Core Principles

These principles guide how skills in this plugin are designed:

1. **Files as Universal Interface** — All output is structured markdown files.
   Self-documenting, inspectable, editable by humans, readable by LLMs. No
   databases, no sync layers.

2. **Primitives + Prompts** — Mechanical work (API calls, parsing) lives in
   scripts. Judgment (what to surface, what to suggest) lives in SKILL.md prompts.
   Behavior changes = prompt edits, not code changes.

3. **Shared Workspace** — The same files are readable and editable by both the
   human and Claude. No sandboxing, no import/export.

4. **context.md Pattern** — Every fetched doc produces an `_index.md` that serves
   as the orientation file. Read `_index.md` first before diving into specific
   tabs or comments.

5. **Composability** — Skills build on each other. The output of `fetch-doc`
   is the input for future skills like `analyze-doc`, `compare-docs`, or
   `summarize-comments`.

6. **Starter-Kit Framing** — When producing proposals, frameworks, or process
   docs for stakeholders, frame them as co-creation starting points rather than
   finished playbooks. Use language like "starting point, not a finished
   playbook" and "let's shape the final version together." Include a built-in
   mechanism for evolution (e.g., retros, review cadences). This avoids the
   "I've decided" anti-pattern and invites ownership from collaborators.

7. **Critique Before Checkpoint** — Multi-phase skills that produce a
   substantive artifact (proposals, PRDs, strategy docs) should run a critique
   pass before presenting to the user. Use `pm:critique` or an inline
   first-principles review to pressure-test the artifact. Iterate on findings
   before the human sees it. This catches structural issues, missing
   perspectives, and unstated assumptions early — reducing round-trips with
   the user.

8. **Match the Voice Around You** — When editing an existing document, match
   the voice and register of the surrounding text. Don't introduce editorial
   commentary, philosophical asides, motivational framing, or defensive
   justifications ("this isn't X, it's Y") unless the doc already uses that
   style. State facts and decisions plainly. When writing new documents on
   behalf of the user, load the style guide and persona — but when editing
   existing docs, the doc itself is the primary style reference.

9. **Concise by Default** — AI output is a draft, never the final artifact.
   Before any document or communication is presented to the user or shared
   with stakeholders, it must pass a conciseness review:
   - **Remove verbosity.** Content should be concise and clear, containing
     useful information and nothing more.
   - **Deduplicate.** If a point is made once, don't restate it. Explicitness
     and clarity are good; repetition and over-explanation are not.
   - **Cut AI-characteristic filler.** Common patterns to eliminate:
     - Hedge stacking: "it's worth noting that perhaps"
     - Unnecessary transitions: "with that in mind", "building on the above"
     - Throat-clearing: "in order to effectively address"
     - Motivational padding: "this powerful approach enables teams to"
     - Restating what was just said in different words
     - Meta-commentary: "I wanted to reach out to..."
   - **Prefer fewer pages.** Multiple readers times inflated text is a
     compounding team cost. Compress ruthlessly.

   This applies to all generated output — documents, proposals, specs,
   communications, and PR descriptions. When in doubt, cut.

10. **Clickable Links for External Resources** — Whenever referencing a PR,
    issue, Google Doc, dashboard, or any external resource, present it as a
    clickable link. For GitHub PRs and issues, use the `owner/repo#123` format
    (renders as a clickable link in most contexts). For URLs, include the full
    URL. Never reference a PR by number alone ("PR 23") or describe a resource
    without linking to it. The user should be able to click through immediately
    without searching.

---

## Stakeholder Document Structure

When producing a document intended for stakeholder review (proposals, specs,
PRDs, process docs, strategy docs, research briefs), use this two-layer
structure:

### At-a-Glance (the main document)
The top section that stakeholders actually read. Requirements:
- **Self-sufficient.** A reader who stops here has everything they need to
  understand the recommendation, make a decision, or give feedback.
- **States decisions and key facts plainly.** No teasers like "see appendix
  for details" — the summary itself must contain the substance.
- **As short as possible.** Target the minimum length where removing another
  sentence would lose essential information.
- **No AI filler.** This section gets the most aggressive conciseness pass.

### Appendix (the reference material)
Everything that supports the at-a-glance section:
- Detailed analysis, evidence, methodology, raw data
- Sources and references
- Extended options considered and rejected
- Full stakeholder feedback or research notes

The appendix exists so readers *can* dig in, not so they *must*. Label it
clearly (e.g., "## Appendix" or "## Supporting Detail") so the boundary
between the two layers is unambiguous.

### When This Applies
- Proposals, specs/PRDs, process docs, strategy docs, research briefs —
  anything going to multiple stakeholders for review
- Does NOT apply to short-form communications (Slack, email, doc comments)
  — those are already concise by nature via `pm:draft-comms`

---

## Doc Output Format Convention

When `pm:fetch-doc` creates a local copy of a Google Doc, it always produces
this structure:

```
{doc-slug}/
  _index.md        <- metadata (YAML frontmatter) + outline + tab map
  tab-{slug}.md    <- one file per tab
  comments.md      <- all comments, grouped by open/resolved
```

### `_index.md` Frontmatter Schema

```yaml
---
title: "Document Title"
doc_id: "1ABC..."
source_url: "https://docs.google.com/document/d/..."
fetched_at: "YYYY-MM-DD"
tabs:
  - title: "Tab Name"
    file: "tab-tab-name.md"
comments:
  open: 3
  resolved: 1
  file: "comments.md"
---
```

### Reading a Fetched Doc

1. Start with `_index.md` — understand what the doc is, what tabs exist, how
   many comments are open
2. Read specific `tab-*.md` files based on what's relevant to the task
3. Check `comments.md` for leadership feedback, open decisions, or action items

Do not read all tab files unless the user asks for a full summary. Large docs
can have 3,000+ lines across tabs — selective loading is the default.

### `drive/` is Read-Only

The `drive/` directory contains fetched Google Doc reference copies. **Never
create or edit files in `drive/`** unless the user explicitly asks. All working
edits go to the canonical file (typically in `projects/` or wherever the user
maintains their working copy). Use `drive/` only as a read source when comparing
against or referencing Google Doc content.

---

## Prerequisites

This plugin requires the Google Workspace CLI (`gws`) to be installed and
authenticated. If `gws` is not available, skills that access Google Docs will
fail with a clear error message.

**Quick check:** Run `which gws` — if it returns a path, you're set.

The `pm:query-db` skill additionally requires `psql` (install via `brew install libpq`),
an SSH key at `~/.ssh/shortform-tasks-2023-01.pem`, and a `.env` file with database
credentials at the product repo root. See the skill's SKILL.md for setup details.

**If not installed:** See the plugin README for full setup instructions.

---

## Optional Tools

### agent-browser

Browser automation CLI ([vercel-labs/agent-browser](https://github.com/vercel-labs/agent-browser)). Use it via the Bash tool for any web task that can't be done through an API — logging into dashboards, exercising product flows in a real browser, scraping pages, filling forms, capturing screenshots. Sessions persist across commands.

**Install (macOS):**

```bash
brew install agent-browser
agent-browser install   # downloads Chrome for Testing, first time only
```

**Core workflow:**

1. `agent-browser open <url>` — navigate to a page
2. `agent-browser snapshot -i` — accessibility tree with interactive refs (`@e1`, `@e2`, …)
3. `agent-browser click @e1` / `agent-browser fill @e2 "text"` — interact using refs
4. Re-snapshot after any page change before the next interaction
5. `agent-browser close` when done

Run `agent-browser --help` for the full command list. For in-depth guidance, install the upstream skill into the working repo with `npx skills add vercel-labs/agent-browser --skill agent-browser --agent claude-code` — it drops a detailed `SKILL.md` at `.claude/skills/agent-browser/` that Claude Code auto-loads.

**When to reach for it vs. other tools:**
- Use APIs first (Mixpanel MCP, `gws`, `gh`, DB). Only use agent-browser when there's no API path.
- Don't use `agent-browser chat` mode — it runs a parallel LLM loop via Vercel AI Gateway, which is redundant when Claude Code is already driving.

**On the skills CLI risk labels:** `npx skills add` surfaces Snyk and Socket scores. For young packages these often reflect heuristic flags (install scripts, short commit history, low popularity) rather than confirmed CVEs — cross-check `security.snyk.io/package/npm/<name>` for the real advisory list before dismissing. For agent-browser (verified 2026-04-10 at [security.snyk.io/package/npm/agent-browser](https://security.snyk.io/package/npm/agent-browser)): zero runtime deps, no known CVEs, and the Homebrew path skips the npm `postinstall` script that triggers the Socket alert.

---

## Git Hygiene for Doc Edits

When editing `.md` files, use git commits as the diff layer so every change is
reviewable after the fact. Claude Code edits bypass VS Code's Timeline and other
editor-based history tools, so git is the only reliable way to see before/after
diffs.

**Guidelines:**
- Before starting a substantive edit session, consider committing the current
  state so there's a clean baseline to diff against
- After each meaningful change, commit with a descriptive message:
  `"Update [doc]: [what changed and why]"`
- To review what a specific edit changed: `git diff HEAD~1 -- path/to/file.md`

See `playbook/git-hygiene-for-docs.md` for full rationale and workflow.

---

## Extending This Plugin

To add a new skill:

1. **Do the work manually first.** Run the workflow by hand in a real session.
   Observe what works, what's awkward, and what the output format should be.
   Iterate with the user until the pattern is clear.
2. **Then codify.** Create a directory: `plugins/ai-native-pm/skills/{skill-name}/`
3. **Add YAML frontmatter to `SKILL.md`.** This is required for Claude Code to
   discover the skill. Without it, the skill won't appear in the system-reminder
   skills list and agents won't know it exists. Use this format:
   ```yaml
   ---
   name: skill-name
   description: What the skill does. Include trigger phrases — words a user might say that should invoke the skill (e.g., "analyze this doc", "wrap up", "capture learnings"). This is what Claude Code uses for auto-invocation.
   tools: Read, Bash, Glob, Grep, Agent
   ---
   ```
   Reference an existing registered skill (e.g., `fetch-doc/SKILL.md`) for examples.
4. Write the `SKILL.md` body following the existing format (phases, prerequisites,
   critical rules). The manual run is your spec — the skill should reproduce
   what you did by hand, not invent a new flow.
5. If the skill needs mechanical work, add a script to
   `plugins/ai-native-pm/tools/`
6. Update this CLAUDE.md to list the new skill in the table above
```

    10→Foundations → Research → Synthesis → Prioritization → PRD → Tech Plan → Tasks → Delivery → Learnings

   196→/playbook:critique docs/foundations/
   197→
   198→# With specific personas
   199→/playbook:critique docs/api-spec/ --personas technical-reviewer,product-manager
   200→
   201→# Re-run after fixes
   202→/playbook:critique docs/foundations/ --rerun
   203→```
   204→
   205→### Learning Capture
   206→Three trigger points for capturing learnings:
   207→- After chat sessions (lightweight)
   208→- After project completion (comprehensive)
   209→- After overcoming blockers (targeted)
   210→
   211→Learnings can improve either the codebase documentation or the plugin itself.
   212→
   213→### External Plugin Integration
   214→Works with other plugins by providing wrapper context when invoking external tools, ensuring outputs conform to playbook workflows.
   215→

   217→The playbook can analyze your coding sessions to identify repeatable patterns and automatically propose new tools:
   218→
   219→```bash
   220→# Analyze recent sessions and improve the playbook
   221→/playbook:improve-playbook
   222→```
   223→
   224→This meta-workflow:
   225→1. **Gathers** sessions from SpecStory history
   226→2. **Analyzes** patterns in how you work
   227→3. **Compares** patterns against existing playbook capabilities
   228→4. **Identifies gaps** where new tools would help
   229→5. **Proposes** solutions (commands, agents, or skills)
   230→6. **Implements** approved improvements
   231→7. **Creates** a pull request

   264→## Usage
   265→
   266→> **Important**: These are slash commands, not skills. Invoke them directly by typing `/playbook:*` in the chat, not via the Skill tool.
   267→
   268→### Complete Workflow Example
   269→
   270→```bash
   271→# 0. (Optional) Build strategy foundations for a new product/strategy
   272→/playbook:foundations
   273→# Create Mission, Vision, Personas, Engagement Framework
   274→# Output: docs/foundations/strategy-foundations.md
   275→
   276→# 0b. (Optional) Synthesize research into opportunities
   277→/playbook:research-synthesis --context docs/research/
   278→# Combine quant + qual + taste research into strategic narrative
   279→# Output: projects/my-feature/research-synthesis.md
   280→
   281→# 0c. (Optional) Prioritize opportunities
   282→# Use resources/templates/prioritization.md to score and rank ideas
   283→# Output: projects/my-feature/prioritization.md
   284→
   285→# 1. Define product requirements
   286→/playbook:product-requirements
   287→# Answer questions, define the problem, users, and success criteria
   288→# Output: projects/my-feature/product-requirements.md
   289→
   290→# 2. Plan the technical approach
   291→/playbook:tech-plan
   292→# Design architecture, sequencing, and technical approach
   293→# Output: projects/my-feature/tech-plan.md
   294→
   295→# 3. Break down into tasks
   296→/playbook:tasks
   297→# Create specific, actionable tasks with acceptance criteria
   298→# Output: projects/my-feature/tasks.md
   299→
   300→# 4. Execute tasks one by one
   301→/playbook:work
   302→# Implement, test, and document each task
   303→# Updates: tasks.md status, code changes
   304→
   305→# 5. Debug any issues
   306→/playbook:debug
   307→# Systematic debugging with verification-first approach
   308→
   309→# 6. Capture learnings
   310→/playbook:learnings
   311→# Document what you learned for future work
   312→# Output: docs/learnings/YYYY-MM-DD-topic.md
   313→```
   314→
   315→### Command Details
   316→
   317→#### `/playbook:foundations`
   318→Builds **strategy foundations** — the decision filters that anchor all downstream product work:
   319→- Creates Mission, Vision, Core Promise, Personas, Engagement Framework
   320→- Each section builds on the previous in a deliberate sequence
   321→- Searches for existing context (company docs, research, interviews)
   322→- Validates internal consistency across the strategy stack
   323→- Foundations become decision filters for PRDs and prioritization
   324→
   325→#### `/playbook:research-synthesis`
   326→Synthesizes research from multiple sources into **strategic opportunities**:
   327→- Three-layer approach: Quantitative + Qualitative + Product Taste
   328→- Cross-layer synthesis finds converging and diverging signals
   329→- Produces ranked opportunities with evidence and confidence ratings
   330→- Bridges "what we know" and "what we should build"
   331→- Sources are tracked for traceability
   332→
   333→#### `/playbook:product-requirements`
   334→Creates **agent-ready PRDs** that enable autonomous technical planning and implementation:
   335→
   336→**Two Modes:**
   337→- **Interview mode** (default): Multi-persona discovery with probing questions
   338→- **Autonomous mode** (`--autonomous`): Drafts complete PRD from available context
   339→
   340→**Agent-Ready Features:**
   341→- Structured acceptance criteria (Given/When/Then format)
   342→- Technical context section (integration points, data, constraints, patterns)
   343→- Decision log with rationale (agents don't re-litigate)
   344→- Explicit scope boundaries (In/Out tables)
   345→- Agent-Ready Checklist validation
   346→
   347→**Why Agent-Ready Matters:**
   348→An AI agent should be able to create a tech plan and complete implementation tasks from the PRD alone—without asking clarifying questions.
   349→
   350→#### `/playbook:tech-plan`
   351→Designs **how** to build it:
   352→- Architecture decisions with rationale
   353→- Sequencing plan (phases, dependencies)
   354→- Technology stack evaluation
   355→- Risk assessment
   356→- Searches for relevant learnings first
   357→
   358→#### `/playbook:tasks`
   359→Breaks work into actionable tasks:
   360→- Acceptance criteria for each task
   361→- Dependencies between tasks
   362→- AI tool recommendations per task
   363→- Effort estimates
   364→
   365→#### `/playbook:work`
   366→Executes tasks systematically:
   367→- Identifies next task based on dependencies
   368→- Searches for relevant learnings before starting
   369→- Quality validation against acceptance criteria
   370→- Updates task status and documentation
   371→
   372→#### `/playbook:debug`
   373→Systematic debugging workflow:
   374→- **Searches for prior solutions first**
   375→- Verification-first approach
   376→- Hypothesis tracking
   377→- Root cause analysis
   378→- Captures learning after resolution
   379→
   380→#### `/playbook:learnings`
   381→Captures learnings with three triggers:
   382→- **Chat session** (lightweight): Quick insights
   383→- **Project completion** (comprehensive): Full retrospective
   384→- **Blocker overcome** (targeted): Problem/solution documentation
   385→
   386→Two output targets:
   387→- **Codebase docs**: Project-specific knowledge
   388→- **Plugin improvements**: Workflow enhancements
   389→
   390→#### `/playbook:improve-playbook`
   391→Meta-workflow that improves the playbook based on your usage patterns:
   392→- **Analyzes** SpecStory session history for repeatable patterns
   393→- **Compares** patterns against existing playbook capabilities
   394→- **Identifies** gaps where new tools would add value
   395→- **Proposes** solutions with evidence from your sessions
   396→- **Implements** approved improvements
   397→- **Creates** a PR to the playbook repository
   398→
   399→Checkpoints throughout ensure you control what gets implemented.
   400→
   401→## Philosophy
   402→
   403→This plugin embodies several key principles:
   404→
   405→- **Multi-Persona Approach**: Different phases benefit from different perspectives
   406→- **Systematic Workflows**: Structured processes reduce cognitive load
   407→- **Learning Compounds**: Captured learnings improve future work
   408→- **Tool Orchestration**: Leverage the best tool for each task
   409→- **Documentation Discipline**: Keep docs updated as you work

```text
# Continuous Improvement Plan: Charter & Persona

**Date**: 2026-03-27
**Status**: Active — starting with manual habit, skill codification later

---

## Philosophy

The charter is a declaration (what I own, believe, and focus on) — it changes when context shifts. The persona is a simulation (how I think and decide) — it compounds with every real decision captured. The persona is where most of the ongoing improvement happens.

---

## Improvement Mechanisms

### 1. Post-Decision Calibration (Start Now — Manual)

After working sessions where Davis makes a real decision, review, or pushback, capture a calibration example:
- **Trigger**: End of any session involving a PRD review, prioritization decision, strategy reaction, or stakeholder pushback
- **Prompt**: "Was there anything in this session worth adding as a calibration example?"
- **Output**: A situation/response/principle triple appended to the persona's Calibration Examples section
- **Cadence**: Every session where a real judgment call was made — not every session

Target: Build from 3 to 8-10 calibration examples over the first 90 days.

### 2. Persona Validation (Monthly)

Run a persona accuracy check by having Claude do work using the persona, then comparing output to Davis's actual reaction:
- Pick a real artifact (PRD, strategy doc, proposal)
- Claude reviews it "as Head of Product" using the persona
- Davis compares: where did Claude get it right? Where did it diverge?
- Divergences become Evolution Log entries and/or corrected heuristics

Research says 3-5 well-chosen corrections dramatically improve accuracy. One check per month is sufficient.

### 3. Charter Drift Detection (Quarterly)

After each quarter's strategy planning, review the charter against reality:
- Did priorities play out as written?
- Did Own/Influence/Don't Own boundaries hold or shift?
- Were any principles violated or refined by real tradeoffs?
- Did the "About Me" growth areas change?

This is a 15-minute review, not a rewrite. Most quarters: 1-2 changes.

### 4. Future Skill Candidates

After 2-3 manual cycles, consider codifying as plugin skills:

**`pm:calibrate-persona`** (high value, simpler)
- Takes a transcript or summary of a real interaction
- Extracts a candidate calibration example
- Presents for approval
- Appends to persona and updates Evolution Log

**`pm:persona-review`** (full vision, more complex)
- Loads persona + artifact
- Produces a simulated review from that POV
- Presents for validation
- Captures divergences as corrections

**`pm:charter-check`** (lightweight, quarterly)
- Surfaces charter-reality gaps during quarterly reviews
- Flags sections that may need updating based on recent work

**Recommendation**: Don't build any of these yet. Establish the manual habit first, then codify once we know what the skill needs to automate.

---

## Example Quality Management

The persona has a target of ~10-15 calibration examples, quality over count (research sweet spot: 3-5, diminishing returns past 15). Once at capacity, new examples should **replace** weaker ones rather than accumulate.

### What Makes an Example Strong

| Quality | Strong Example | Weak Example |
|---------|---------------|--------------|
| **Surprising** | Reveals a judgment call that couldn't be predicted from stated values | Confirms what the persona already says |
| **Corrective** | Shows where an agent would have guessed wrong | Validates the obvious |
| **Distinctive** | Captures something specific to Davis, not any generic PM | Could describe any Head of Product |
| **Covers new ground** | Represents a situation type not already covered | Duplicates an existing situation type |
| **Observed** | From a real interaction or decision | Inferred during onboarding interview |

### Replacement Process

When a new candidate example emerges during calibration:

1. **Classify the situation type.** Does it cover a type we already have? (e.g., prioritization, stakeholder feedback, resource request, language/values, opportunity identification)
2. **If new type → check capacity.** If under 15 examples, add it. If at 15, identify the weakest existing example to replace.
3. **If existing type → compare directly.** Is the new example sharper, more surprising, or from a real interaction (vs. inferred)? If yes, replace. If not, skip.
4. **Weakest-first replacement priority:**
   - Interview-inferred examples (examples 1-3) are first candidates for replacement — they bootstrapped the persona but will be outclassed by real interaction data
   - Examples that are redundant with another example of the same type
   - Examples that confirm stated values without adding nuance

### Periodic Audit (Quarterly, alongside Charter Drift Detection)

Review all calibration examples and ask:
- Are any examples now redundant with each other?
- Do any examples describe behavior that has since changed?
- Are there recent uncaptured interactions that would be stronger than existing examples?
- Is the distribution across situation types balanced, or are we over-indexed on one type?

Propose swaps and get Davis's approval before making changes.

---

## Compound Learning Flow

Inspired by [compound-engineering](https://github.com/EveryInc/compound-engineering-plugin) (`/ce:compound`) and [product-playbook](https://github.com/daviswhitehead/product-playbook-for-agentic-coding-plugin) (`learning-capture`), this section defines how persona and charter learnings compound across sessions.

### Three Trigger Points

| Trigger | When | Duration | What to Capture |
|---------|------|----------|----------------|
| **Post-session** (lightweight) | End of any session with a real decision or reaction | 2-5 min | Calibration examples, heuristic corrections, evolution log entries |
| **Post-project** (comprehensive) | After completing a major deliverable (PRD, strategy doc, experiment design) | 10-15 min | Full persona review — are examples still accurate? Any new patterns? Charter still aligned? |
| **Post-correction** (targeted) | When Davis says "that's not how I'd think about it" or the persona produces an inaccurate simulation | 5 min | Evolution log entry + corrected heuristic or replaced example |

### Two Output Targets

| Target | What Gets Updated | Format |
|--------|-------------------|--------|
| **Persona file** | Calibration examples, decision heuristics, known biases, evolution log | Direct edits to `context/personas/head-of-product.md` |
| **Scratch files** | Observations about other stakeholders (Allen, Boris, etc.) for future personas | Appended to `context/personas/founder-ceo-scratch.md` (or new scratch files) |

### Capture Workflow

```
1. Trigger detected (session end, project complete, or correction)
     ↓
2. Claude surfaces candidates
   - Reviews session for decisions, reactions, pushback, surprises
   - Proposes calibration examples as situation/response/principle triples
   - Flags any observations about other stakeholders for scratch files
     ↓
3. Davis evaluates each candidate
   - "Accurate" → proceed to step 4
   - "Adjust" → Davis corrects, Claude rewrites
   - "Skip" → move to next candidate
     ↓
4. Quality check against existing examples
   - New situation type? → Add if under 10, else replace weakest
   - Existing situation type? → Replace only if new is stronger
   - Redundant with stated values? → Skip
     ↓
5. Write to persona file
   - Append/replace calibration example
   - Update evolution log with date, change, and source
   - If heuristic correction: update Values & Decision Heuristics section
     ↓
6. (Optional) Flag for charter update
   - If the learning reveals a shift in priorities, ownership, or principles
   - Queue for next charter review rather than updating immediately
```

### Auto-Detection Signals

Claude should proactively surface calibration candidates when it notices:
- Davis pushing back on a recommendation ("no, because...")
- Davis reframing a question or rejecting terminology
- Davis making a sequencing or prioritization call with stated reasoning
- Davis expressing surprise or strong reaction to data
- Davis making a decision that contradicts what the persona would predict
- A gap between Claude's framing and Davis's actual perspective

### What NOT to Capture

- Generic confirmations ("yes, that looks good") — no signal
- Operational details (meeting times, tool access) — not persona-relevant
- One-off tactical decisions that won't generalize
- Anything already well-represented in the existing examples

---

## Post-Decision Hook (Lightweight Implementation)

### What It Is

A brief prompt at the end of working sessions that captures persona-improving signal without interrupting flow. Not a formal process — just a consistent closing question.

### How It Works

At natural session endpoints (after a decision is made, a doc is reviewed, a strategy is discussed), Claude asks:

> **Quick calibration check:** Anything from this session worth capturing for your persona? Could be a decision you made and why, a reaction that surprised you, or a place where my framing didn't match how you'd actually think about it. One sentence is enough — I'll write it up.

Davis can respond in one of three ways:
- **A quick note**: "Yeah — when I saw X, my reaction was Y because Z." → Claude writes the calibration example and appends it.
- **A correction**: "You framed that wrong — I wouldn't prioritize it that way because..." → Claude writes an Evolution Log entry and adjusts the relevant heuristic.
- **Skip**: "Nothing this time." → Move on. No friction.

### When to Trigger

Not every session. Use judgment based on whether the session involved:
- A real prioritization or sequencing decision
- A review where Davis pushed back or got excited about something specific
- A moment where Davis's actual reaction differed from what the persona would predict
- A new principle or heuristic that emerged from the work

### What Good Calibration Looks Like

**Good** (captures a non-obvious judgment call):
> "I deprioritized the referral program even though it has high long-term upside, because right now we don't even retain the users we have. Fix the bucket before you pour more water in."

**Less useful** (too generic, already captured in the persona):
> "I liked the data-driven approach."

Prioritize examples that are **surprising, non-obvious, or corrective** — not ones that confirm what the persona already says.

---

# Socratic Questioning

## What It Is

A structured questioning framework that prevents jumping to solutions before fully understanding the problem. Instead of asserting answers, it uses targeted questions to expose assumptions, clarify thinking, and validate direction.

## When to Use

- Writing or reviewing a PRD
- Evaluating a feature proposal before committing resources
- Challenging your own assumptions about a problem space
- Preparing for a stakeholder review where hard questions will come up

## The Framework

### 1. Problem Clarity

- What specific pain are we solving?
- Who experiences this pain, and how often?
- What's the cost of inaction — what happens if we do nothing?
- How do we know this is a real problem vs. an assumed one?

### 2. Solution Validation

- Why this solution over alternatives?
- What's the simplest version that would still solve the problem?
- What are we assuming about user behavior?
- What have we tried before, and what did we learn?

### 3. Success Criteria

- How will we measure success?
- What metric movement would make this a failure?
- What's the target within what timeframe?
- Can we detect success/failure quickly, or does it take months?

### 4. Constraints

- What are the top technical risks?
- What are we explicitly NOT doing?
- If we had half the time, what would we cut?
- What dependencies could block us?

### 5. Strategic Fit

- Why now — what changed that makes this the right time?
- How does this connect to broader company strategy?
- What does this enable or block in the future?
- How does this affect our competitive position?

## How to Apply

Pick 3-5 of the most relevant questions per use. Quality over quantity — a focused set of questions produces better thinking than running through all 20. Start with Problem Clarity if you're early in discovery; start with Solution Validation if a solution has already been proposed.
```
</details></tool-use>

# Rumelt's Strategy Kernel

## What It Is

Richard Rumelt's framework for distinguishing real strategy from aspirational goals. A strategy kernel has three parts — a diagnosis, a guiding policy, and coherent actions — that form a logical chain from understanding a challenge to addressing it.

## When to Use

- Creating or reviewing a product strategy
- Evaluating whether a "strategy" is actually a strategy or just a list of goals
- Aligning a team on what you're doing and — critically — what you're not doing
- Preparing a strategy presentation for leadership

## The Framework

### 1. Diagnosis

A clear, actionable statement of the strategic challenge, grounded in data. Not a restatement of goals ("we need to grow") but an identification of the obstacle or opportunity ("our trial-to-paid conversion drops 40% when users don't complete onboarding within 48 hours").

**Test**: Could someone disagree with this diagnosis? If not, it's too vague.

### 2. Guiding Policy

The overall approach for dealing with the challenge identified in the diagnosis. Must include explicit tradeoffs — what you're choosing NOT to do.

**Test**: Does this policy rule things out? A guiding policy that's compatible with everything is not a policy.

### 3. Coherent Actions

Specific, coordinated initiatives that implement the guiding policy. "Coherent" means they reinforce each other rather than pulling in different directions.

**Test**: Do these actions support the guiding policy, or are some of them just good ideas unrelated to the diagnosis?

### The "Strategy vs. Non-Strategy" Test

These are NOT strategies:

- **Goals**: "Grow revenue 30%" (that's an objective, not a strategy)
- **Feature lists**: "Build X, Y, Z" (those are actions without a diagnosis or guiding policy)
- **Visions**: "Be the best X in the world" (that's an aspiration)
- **Values**: "We believe in quality" (that's a principle, not a strategy)

A real strategy connects all three elements: here's the challenge (diagnosis), here's our approach (guiding policy), here's what we'll do (coherent actions).

## How to Apply

1. Start with the diagnosis — what's the real challenge? Use data, not platitudes.
2. Derive a guiding policy that addresses that specific challenge. State what you're NOT doing.
3. List coherent actions that implement the policy. Check that they reinforce each other.
4. Run the "non-strategy" test: if you removed any of the three elements, would it still make sense? If yes, the chain is too loose.
```
</details></tool-use>

# Impact Estimation

## What It Is

A quantitative framework for estimating the impact of a product change before building it. Uses a formula-based approach with three scenarios to bound uncertainty and make assumptions explicit.

## When to Use

- Prioritizing features or experiments (RICE scoring, roadmap planning)
- Building a business case for a proposal
- Comparing the expected value of competing options
- Pressure-testing intuitive "this will be big" claims with numbers

## The Framework

### The Core Formula

```
Impact = Users Affected x Current Action Rate x Expected Lift x Value per Action
```

Each variable should be estimated independently with a source citation.

### Three-Scenario Analysis

| Scenario | Adoption | Lift | When to Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| Pessimistic (20th percentile) | 30% of eligible users | Conservative — lowest credible improvement | Default for commitments and timelines |
| Realistic (50th percentile) | 70% of eligible users | Moderate — most likely outcome | Default for planning and prioritization |
| Optimistic (80th percentile) | 90% of eligible users | Best-case + retention effects | Use to size upside and justify exploration |

### Lift Estimation Source Hierarchy

Ranked by reliability:

1. **Historical data** — Your own past experiments with similar changes (most reliable)
2. **User research** — Direct evidence from interviews, surveys, usability tests
3. **Competitor benchmarks** — Published case studies or teardowns of similar features
4. **Expert judgment** — Team intuition calibrated by experience (least reliable, but sometimes all you have)

Always note which source level you're using. A "2x lift" from historical data is a different confidence level than a "2x lift" from expert judgment.

## How to Apply

1. Define each variable in the formula with real numbers from your product
2. Run all three scenarios
3. Present the realistic scenario as the headline, with pessimistic/optimistic as the range
4. Flag which variables have the weakest evidence — those are your biggest risks
5. If the pessimistic scenario still justifies the investment, it's a strong bet
```
</details></tool-use>

# Devil's Advocate Protocol

## What It Is

A structured approach for pressure-testing decisions by generating the strongest possible counter-arguments. The goal is not to be contrarian but to surface risks and assumptions that groupthink or momentum might obscure.

## When to Use

- After making a strategic choice but before committing resources
- When a decision feels "obvious" — that's when hidden assumptions are most dangerous
- Before presenting a recommendation to leadership
- When a team has converged too quickly on a solution

## The Framework

### Step 1: State the Decision Clearly

Write the decision as a specific, falsifiable statement: "We will [action] because [reason], expecting [outcome]."

### Step 2: Challenge Each Assumption

For each key assumption, generate the strongest counter-argument:

- **Market timing**: "What if this market window doesn't exist, or has already closed?"
- **Competitive response**: "What will competitors do when they see this? Could they neutralize our advantage?"
- **Resource sufficiency**: "Do we actually have the people, time, and money to execute this well?"
- **User behavior**: "Are we assuming users will do something they've never done before?"
- **Opportunity cost**: "What are we NOT doing by choosing this? Is that trade-off worth it?"

### Step 3: Defend or Reconsider

For each counter-argument:

- If you can defend the original decision with evidence, it's stronger for having been tested
- If you can't defend it, you've found a real risk that needs mitigation or a reason to reconsider
- If you're uncertain, that's a signal to gather more data before committing

### Step 4: Document the Result

Record: the decision, the strongest counter-arguments raised, your defense or adjustment for each. This becomes an artifact that shows rigorous thinking.

## How to Apply

Better to hear hard questions from AI than from your CEO. Run this protocol:

- On any proposal before it goes to leadership
- On strategic bets where the downside of being wrong is significant
- After a brainstorming session when energy is high but scrutiny is low
- As a complement to `pm:critique` — devil's advocate focuses on the decision logic while critique focuses on stakeholder perspectives
```
</details></tool-use>

```text
# Executive Briefing

**Audience**: Leadership / executives
**Tone**: Concise, confident, data-grounded
**Length**: 200-400 words (3 paragraphs)

## Structure

### Paragraph 1: Context
Set up the situation in 2-3 sentences. What's happening, why it matters now, and what data triggered this briefing. No background the reader already knows.

### Paragraph 2: Key Findings
The core insight or analysis. Lead with the most important number or conclusion. Include supporting data points. Keep to 3-5 sentences max.

### Paragraph 3: Recommended Action
What you're proposing and what you need from the reader (approval, resources, a decision). Be specific about the ask. Include timeline if relevant.

## Example

Trial-to-paid conversion dropped from 14.2% to 11.8% over the past 6 weeks, coinciding with our shift to shorter trial periods. This represents ~$45K/month in lost revenue at current trial volume.

Analysis of cohort data shows the drop is concentrated in users who don't complete onboarding within the first 48 hours — their conversion rate fell from 9% to 4%, while users who complete onboarding held steady at 22%. The shorter trial gives less time to recover late-onboarding users who previously converted in week 2.

I recommend we run a 2-week experiment extending trials to 10 days for users who haven't completed onboarding by day 3, while keeping the 7-day trial for active users. Engineering estimates 3 days of work. I'd like approval to prioritize this in the current sprint.

## Anti-patterns

- **Burying the ask**: The recommended action should be explicit and near the end, not implied
- **Including implementation details**: Executives don't need to know which API endpoint changes — they need to know the impact and the cost
- **Hedging language**: "We might want to consider possibly looking into..." — state your recommendation directly
- **Too much background**: If the reader already knows the context, skip it. Start with what's new.
- **No numbers**: Every executive briefing should have at least one concrete metric
```
</details></tool-use>

```text
---
name: close
description: Run a session close-out — checks for uncommitted work, cleans up tasks, captures memory, writes handoff context, then runs the learn flow. Use when the user says "let's wrap up", "I'm done for today", "closing out", "anything else before I go?", or at the end of a substantive working session. Triggers on "close", "wrap up", "done for today", "closing out", "end of session".
tools: Read, Edit, Write, Bash, Glob, Grep, Agent
---

**Recommended mode**: Auto-Accept
**Thinking depth**: Normal

# Claude Code: pm:close Workflow

This skill runs at the end of a working session to ensure nothing is lost — uncommitted work, incomplete tasks, uncaptured learnings, or context that the next session will need. It's a lightweight orchestrator that sequences mechanical checks before knowledge capture.

## When to Use

### Explicit Invocation
Use `/pm:close` when:
- User says "let's wrap up", "I'm done for today", "closing out this session"
- User says "anything else before I go?"
- At the end of any substantive working session

### Proactive Invocation
Claude should proactively suggest closing when it detects:
- User signaling session end ("thanks, that's all", "I'm heading out", "let's stop here")
- A natural stopping point after completing the last requested task
- A long session (2+ hours of active work) approaching a natural break

**Proactive trigger format:**
> Before you go — want me to run a quick close-out? I'll check for uncommitted work, clean up tasks, and capture any learnings.

User can say yes, skip specific phases, or decline entirely.

## Inputs

```
/pm:close                    # Full close-out
/pm:close --quick            # Skip learn flow, just do mechanical checks
/pm:close --learn-only       # Skip mechanical checks, just run learn
```

## Workflow Phases

### Phase 1: Uncommitted Work Check

1. **Run `git status`** in the current working directory (and any active worktrees).
2. If there are uncommitted changes:
   - Show a brief summary of what's changed (files modified, added, deleted)
   - Ask: "Want to commit these before closing? I can draft a commit message."
   - If yes, draft a commit message and commit
   - If no, note what's uncommitted so the next session knows
3. If clean, skip silently.

### Phase 2: Task Cleanup

1. **Check for active tasks** (TaskList).
2. For each task still marked `in_progress`:
   - Is the work actually done? If yes, mark completed.
   - Is the work partially done? Note what's left for the next session.
   - Is the task stale (from an earlier part of the session that's no longer relevant)? Propose deletion.
3. For tasks marked `pending` that weren't started:
   - Note them as carryover for the next session.
4. Present a brief summary of task state. Don't belabor it — just flag anything that needs attention.

### Phase 2.5: Project Status Check

1. **Check if any project folder's status has changed** based on the session's work.
   - Did a project move from `in-progress` to `complete`? (e.g., all deliverables shipped, experiment concluded)
   - Did a project move from `in-progress` to a different state?
2. If a status change is detected, propose moving the project folder:
   > The q2-spec-writing project appears complete — move `projects/in-progress/q2-spec-writing/` to `projects/complete/q2-spec-writing/`?
3. Only move with user approval. If no status changes, skip silently.

### Phase 3: Memory Check

1. **Scan the session** for information worth persisting to auto-memory that isn't persona/charter signal (that's handled by learn). Look for:
   - **Project decisions** — "we decided to do X because Y" that future sessions need to know
   - **Reference pointers** — "the data lives in X", "ask Y about Z", "this doc is at URL"
   - **Feedback/preferences** — "don't do X", "always do Y", working style corrections
   - **User context** — new information about the user's role, responsibilities, or knowledge

2. If candidates exist, present them briefly:
   > **Memory candidate:** You mentioned that Mixpanel MCP access needs to be enabled on project 1961177 before analytics queries will work. Save this as a reference memory?

3. Get approval before writing. If nothing worth saving, skip silently.

### Phase 4: Handoff Context

1. **Draft a brief handoff note** that captures what a fresh session would need to pick up where this one left off. Include:
   - **What was accomplished** this session (2-3 bullets)
   - **What's in progress** but not finished (if anything)
   - **What's next** — the most logical next action based on where things stand
   - **Key decisions made** that affect future work
   - **Open questions** that still need answers

2. **Detect multi-phase projects.** If the session worked on a project with a
   defined plan (e.g., a `plan.md` with numbered phases, a phased workflow, or
   explicit checkpoints), add phase-aware context to the handoff:
   - **Phase completed**: Which phase finished this session (e.g., "Phase 1/5:
     Definitions")
   - **Blocker for next phase**: What must happen before the next phase can start
     (e.g., "Davis must review and approve definitions before Phase 2")
   - **Parallelizable work**: Any downstream work that can proceed without
     waiting for the blocker (e.g., "Phase 4 external research can start in
     parallel with Phase 2")
   - **Next phase scope**: Brief description of what the next phase involves so
     the next session can estimate effort

   This prevents the next session from re-reading the full plan to understand
   where things stand. If the session was single-phase or ad-hoc, skip this
   silently.

3. Present the handoff note to the user. They can adjust, approve, or skip.

4. If approved, save to `projects/{active-project}/handoff-{date}.md` if there's an active project, or present it inline for the user to reference when starting the next session.

### Phase 5: Learn Flow

**IMPORTANT: This phase is NOT optional.** Unless the user explicitly passes
`--quick` or the learn flow already ran this session, always run the full
`pm:learn` flow. Do not skip it to save time. Do not shortcut to the summary.
The learn flow captures plugin improvement opportunities and persona signal
that compound across sessions — skipping it means losing learnings permanently.

**Depth matters as much as running it.** A shallow learn pass (one feedback memory, no stakeholder or tooling scan) is almost as bad as skipping. The learn skill's completeness gate ensures all signal categories are checked. If you find yourself presenting just 1-2 candidates from a substantive session, you haven't looked hard enough.

1. **Run the full `pm:learn` flow** (Phases 1-7 from the learn SKILL.md).
   - If `--quick` flag was used, skip this phase entirely.
   - If the learn flow was already run earlier in the session (user explicitly invoked `/pm:learn`), skip and note: "Learn flow already ran this session."

2. **The learn flow must start by diffing agent output against the user's final
   edits.** This is the highest-signal source of learnings. Every manual edit
   the user made is implicit feedback. Do not skip this step or treat it as
   optional. A learn flow that only scans for "did anything break?" without
   examining what the user changed will produce shallow, generic observations.

3. **For every learning candidate, check system-of-record files.** A memory is
   a stopgap. If the learning is a style preference, update the style guide.
   If it's a reusable pattern, update the relevant skill doc. If it's a
   project convention, update CLAUDE.md. Propose concrete changes, not just
   observations.

4. **The mechanical checks (Phases 1-4) protect the current session's work. The learn flow protects compounding returns across all future sessions.** Skipping it means stakeholder signal, persona calibration, and tooling improvements are lost permanently — there's no way to recover session context after the conversation ends.

### Phase 6: Summary

Present a concise close-out summary:

```
✓ Session closed

Git: [Committed 3 files / Clean / 2 files uncommitted (noted for next session)]
Tasks: [3 completed, 1 carried forward, 2 deleted (stale)]
Memory: [1 reference saved / Nothing new]
Handoff: [Saved to projects/charter-persona-setup/handoff-2026-04-01.md]
Learn: [2 persona examples added, 1 stakeholder observation saved / Already ran / Skipped]
```

## Critical Rules

- **Fast phases first.** Git check, task cleanup, and memory are fast. Learn is slow. Always do mechanical checks before knowledge capture so the user's work is safe quickly.
- **Don't block on learn.** If the user is in a hurry, `--quick` skips the learn flow entirely. Uncommitted work and task cleanup are more important than capturing learnings — you can always learn next session, but you can't recover uncommitted work.
- **Don't repeat learn.** If `/pm:learn` was already run this session, don't run it again. Just note it in the summary.
- **Handoff context is for the next session, not for humans.** Write it in a way that's useful for Claude picking up context in a fresh conversation — specific file paths, decision rationale, open questions with enough context to understand them without the conversation history.
- **Keep it lightweight.** The whole close-out should take 3-5 minutes for a routine session. If there's a lot to capture, the learn flow will take longer, but the mechanical checks should always be fast.
- **Don't force any phase.** If git is clean, skip silently. If there are no tasks, skip silently. If there's nothing for memory, skip silently. Only surface what actually needs attention.
```
</details></tool-use>
